content:
Everyone wants the "secret AI stack." There isn't one. There's just the boring list nobody screenshots.

Here's the whole thing SkynetLabs runs on, under $200/mo:

Claude Code · Cursor · n8n (self-hosted) · GHL · Hostinger · Vercel · Supabase · Resend

That's it. 50+ workflows and 1-2 client builds a week come out of those eight tools. No $2K enterprise seats, no headcount.

The leverage was never the tool. It was deleting everything that wasn't on this list.

What's the one tool you pay for that you could kill this week?

#ClaudeCode #n8n #Solopreneur #AIagency #BuildInPublic

I told myself for 18 months that I needed a team.

A junior dev. A designer. A VA. A "growth person." A whole org chart for an agency I hadn't built yet.

Then I just started shipping. Here's what actually does the work now:

→ Claude Code does the dev. I review, refine, ship.
→ Cursor + a saved component library does the design. I taste-check.
→ n8n + GHL run the inbox triage and the follow-ups.
→ Subagents do the research, the draft copy, the QA passes.

The role I kept imagining outsourcing? Most of it didn't need a human. The judgment layer — what to build, what to charge, who to say no to — stays with me. Everything else compresses into AI + workflow.

I'm not anti-team. Scaling to multiple verticals? Hire. A partner whose instincts complement yours? Hire. But for the 0-to-$15K-MRR solo stage, the team is a story you tell yourself to delay shipping.

What's the role you almost hired for that AI ended up doing better?

#Solopreneur #AIagency #ClaudeAI #BuildInPublic

A client asked: "Can your bot handle French, Arabic and Lingala on WhatsApp, qualify the lead, and route to a human when it spots buying intent?"

Three years ago that was a $40K, 3-month build. We shipped it in 5 days.

The stack:
→ n8n self-hosted on a $15 VPS
→ WhatsApp Cloud API
→ Claude for the language layer (50+ languages natively, no separate translation step)
→ Supabase for state

The detail most builders miss: WhatsApp's 2026 policy bans open-ended "ask me anything" bots. So it isn't free chat — it's a 4-question qualify flow. Hot lead → human. Cold → nurture. Wrong number → polite exit.

If you're not building these for the SMBs in your network, someone else will be.

#WhatsAppAPI #n8n #ClaudeAI #LeadGen #AIagency

RIP Zapier $2K/mo enterprise plans.

I'm being a little dramatic — Zapier isn't dying, it's a great on-ramp for non-technical operators. But the segment paying $1-3K/mo for 50K+ task workflows is migrating fast. I've talked to four ops directors in 60 days who all ran the same math.

What replaces it:
→ n8n self-hosted, $15-30/mo for the VPS
→ Claude API for the AI nodes — pennies per call
→ A weekend rebuilding the 5-10 workflows that handle 80% of the volume
→ Better data residency, better debug surface, better unit economics

The migration script that's worked for me three times:
1. List every Zap, sort by tasks/month.
2. Migrate the top 5 by volume first.
3. Run them parallel for two weeks. Validate.
4. Once stable, cancel Zapier and move the long tail at leisure.

If you've made this switch — what was the first workflow you moved, and what broke?

#Zapier #n8n #Automation #AIagency #BuildInPublic

Builders read this. Packagers will hate it.

Most "AI agency" content sounds identical because the people writing it aren't building anything. They're packaging. That's a real job — but when packaging masquerades as building, the content goes shallow and the audience feels it.

The tells:
→ Builders name the actual tool. Packagers say "the right AI stack."
→ Builders show a real workflow with real failure modes. Packagers show a 4-step diagram with vague arrows.
→ Builders cite a number — 14,200 runs, $0.30 per execution, a 5-day sprint. Packagers say "10x" and "scale."
→ Builders post an L on something they shipped. Packagers post a testimonial nobody can verify.

Builder content has a smaller audience and a higher conversion. The DMs come in with technical questions instead of "how do I get started in AI?"

If you're a builder, write like one. Specific, opinionated, technical. Your people will engage.

#BuildInPublic #ClaudeAI #Solopreneur #AIagency

If I restarted my agency today, here's what I'd do differently in the first 30 days.

Skip:
→ Make / Zapier / Pipedream as the primary stack. Great for prototypes, not for a margin-sustainable business in 2026.
→ Retainer-first pricing. The lock-in feels safe until cash flow tightens and you realize it's just a slower hourly.
→ Custom proposals. Every one is unpaid sales labor that slows the close.
→ "Generalist agency" positioning. "Agency" means nothing to a buyer. "Specialist" closes.

Ship:
→ One productized offer in week one. Fixed scope, fixed price, $1.5-2K.
→ A working demo of that offer, before the first sales call.
→ A scoping doc with 5-8 written questions, answered before any call.
→ A 5-day delivery sprint, 50/50 payment.
→ A simple referral message at the end of every project.

My first-time mistake was treating the agency like a buffet — five offers, no specialty, slow close. The fix was one productized service and refusing everything else for 90 days.

What's the move you wish you'd made in your first 30 days?

#AIagency #Productized #Solopreneur #ClaudeAI #BuildInPublic

I tried replacing my entire CRM with one Claude agent reading a single markdown file.

It worked beautifully — until 47 active contacts.

What broke first:
→ Token economics. By contact 35, every "what's pending" query was 40K+ tokens.
→ Memory drift. With 50+ entries in context, Claude conflated two different contacts named Sarah. Subtle, but every conflation cost trust.
→ Update integrity. Edit a contact mid-conversation, ask a follow-up, and sometimes Claude reasoned over the pre-edit version.
→ Search precision. Semantic search is great for "the lead from real estate." It's bad at "the lead with the $850 budget."

The fix wasn't "buy a CRM." It was hybridize:
→ Structured fields (budget, timeline, status) → a Supabase table
→ Long-form notes and context → stayed in markdown
→ Claude reads both via MCP

Now it scales past 100 contacts. The question was never "AI or database" — it's "what's the right shape for each type of data?"

What's the failure point you hit first replacing a CRM with AI?

#ClaudeAI #CRM #MCP #DataModeling #BuildInPublic

Small win. Posting because nobody talks about this.

I'd been paying $97/mo for a SaaS that emailed me a daily digest of LinkedIn hashtags I track. Genuinely useful tool.

Then Claude Routines went GA. I rebuilt the same workflow as a single Routine: scrape 12 hashtags daily at 7am, summarize into 3 themes, email me. Setup: 15 minutes. Cost: included in my $20/mo Max plan. Annual saving: $1,164.

The bigger point: every solo operator has $300-800/mo in SaaS that exists only because there was no scheduling layer on top of an LLM. There is now.

Audit your stack this weekend. For each tool ask: "is this a database + UI, or a prompt + cron?" The second category is now a 15-minute Routine. The first stays.

I'm tracking 6 more subs to kill this month.

#ClaudeAI #Productivity #SaaS #Solopreneur #BuildInPublic

I drew the entire delivery flow that produced 50 public GitHub repos on a single whiteboard. Six nodes.

→ Node 1: Inbound (Fiverr brief, Upwork RFP, DM, referral)
→ Node 2: Scoping (5-8 written questions, async)
→ Node 3: Spec demo (Vercel preview, sometimes built before the call)
→ Node 4: Build sprint (Claude Code + Cursor, 3-5 days, daily Loom)
→ Node 5: Handoff (deployed URL, repo transfer, 14-day tuning window)
→ Node 6: Referral hook (one message at the end)

The 50 repos exist because Node 4 ran 50 times. No mystery.

What's interesting is what's NOT on the board:
→ No "client management" node — the 5-8 question doc replaces 80% of it.
→ No "marketing" node — referrals + case studies handle inbound. No paid ads.
→ No "sales" node — the spec demo IS the sale.

When the system fits on one board, you can iterate on it. When it spreads across 4 tools and 6 Notion pages, it ossifies.

How many nodes is your version?

#AIagency #Productized #Solopreneur #BuildInPublic #ClaudeCode

If you sell websites or automations to clinics, the compliance conversation just got easier — and it's an opening, not a barrier.

I built a Next.js site for a UK Clinical Lead Nurse last month. CQC mapping, GDPR, real first-person voice instead of corporate "we." It went up as a speculative demo before the pitch. The pitch landed; we're working through 7 scope questions now.

What I didn't expect from the niche:
→ Most clinic sites still use stock photos and "trusted by thousands" filler. Clinical staff hate it — they tell me directly in scoping calls.
→ Inspectors actually look at the website. An owner who knows that values pages mapped to evidence requirements.
→ Most "clinic web design" charges $5-15K for slow CMS builds with no compliance scaffolding.

Three angles that resonate:
1. "Your compliance officer can audit this site in 20 minutes."
2. "Real photos of your real staff, with proper consent flows."
3. "Inquiries route to qualified leads, not your front-desk inbox."

If you know a UK or US clinic owner who needs a real site instead of a template, comment "CLINIC" and I'll send the demo + scope.

#Healthcare #ClinicMarketing #NextJS #ComplianceFirst #AIagency

I expected Claude Routines to be a toy. A week later, three of my production workflows run on it. Not prototypes — production.

Which ones moved over:
→ Daily content brief (research news → write the script). 90% of the value was the prompt, not the orchestration.
→ Weekly competitor scan (read 10 LinkedIn pages → summarize what changed in their offer).
→ Email triage (read inbox → flag the 3 highest-priority threads with one-line context).

Which ones stayed in n8n:
→ Anything with branching that depends on output ("if X route to A, if Y route to B").
→ Anything touching external webhooks (Stripe, GHL, Calendly).
→ Anything where the client needs an audit trail of every execution.

The sharp lesson: I was conflating "the workflow" with "the workflow tool." A real chunk of what I'd built in n8n was just "I needed a place to schedule a prompt." Routines is that place now.

What did you assume needed n8n that turned out to be one prompt and a scheduler?

#ClaudeAI #ClaudeRoutines #n8n #Automation #BuildInPublic

Saying the quiet part out loud.

Most "AI agencies" on this app are one operator, a laptop, and a Stripe account. No team. No office. No fancy onboarding deck. That's not a bug in the model — that's the whole model.

The labor compression from Claude + n8n + Vercel collapsed the org chart. The 8-person 2018 agency — junior dev, designer, account manager, ops, two seniors, a strategist, a founder — is now one person who can taste, scope, ship, and own the relationship, with AI doing the labor.

Why it's good news:
→ The barrier to start is under $200/mo and one productized offer.
→ Margins on a well-priced productized service run 60-80%, sustainable solo.
→ The work-life model beats VC-backed agency life. Fewer meetings, more shipping.

Why it scares incumbents:
→ A solo operator can deliver a $5K build for $1.5K at the same quality.
→ The "agency moat" was always headcount and process. AI compressed both.

If you're building, this is the era. Where's this read wrong? Push back specifically.

#AI #AIagency #FutureOfWork #Solopreneur #ClaudeAI

I don't edit our reels anymore. I prompt them. 🎬

The bali nomad vlog that scored 90/100 on our critic loop? One prompt. The cinematic sunset reel? One prompt. The client demo promos, the comment-bait magnets, the faceless explainers — all of it starts as a master prompt I paste into Claude Code.

So I wrote down all 17 video-editing skills. Each one is a complete, tested prompt — not theory.

I'm giving away the whole library. Drop your email once, unlock everything, and get every new prompt as we ship it.

Grab all 17 free → skynetjoe.com/edit-videos-with-claude
